figures.gloss: She who fell from the Sky-World through the hole where the great tree was uprooted, caught on the wings of waterfowl and set upon the back of the Great Turtle; on the mud the muskrat died bringing up she danced the island wide, and from her line came the people and the world.
figures.facet: Creation as a falling and a catching, not a command: the world begins with a woman who descends — pregnant, grieving, received by animals who choose to save her — so that the first act of the cosmos is hospitality and the first author of the earth is a guest, not a king.
figures.provenance: The fullest versions come through 17th-c. Jesuit Relations (Brébeuf) and later Seneca/Onondaga tellings recorded by J.N.B. Hewitt (himself Tuscarora); details of Ataentsic's name and role vary across the Six Nations.

figures.gloss: The turtle who offered its back as the ground of the world, upon which the dived-up earth was spread; the continent itself is Turtle Island, and the land is understood to rest, still, upon the living shell of a patient beast.
figures.facet: The earth as a creature's body kept aloft by consent: the foundation of the world is not stone or pillar but an animal that agreed to bear it — cosmology grounded in a covenant between humankind and the more-than-human, the very land a relationship rather than a possession.

figures.gloss: When Sky Woman fell to a world all water, the animals dived in turn for the bottom — beaver, otter, loon, each failing — until the humble muskrat went down and surfaced dead, but with a paw of mud, and that pinch of earth, laid on the turtle, grew into all the land.
figures.facet: The world saved by the least and at the cost of a life: it is the small, overlooked muskrat who succeeds where the strong divers fail, and he succeeds by dying — the earth bought with the breath of the humblest creature, a cosmogony of self-spending rather than power.

figures.gloss: The good-minded twin, grandson of Sky Woman, who made the gentle things — the straight rivers, the ripe fruit, the deer and the maize and the human form; born the right way, he shapes a world fitted to life and teaches the people the arts of living well.
figures.facet: The maker as one of a contending pair: the benevolent order of the world is not decreed from above but won, piece by piece, against a brother who unmakes — every good thing the Good Mind makes is answered and worried by Flint, so that the lived world is the standing truce of their long argument.

figures.gloss: The flint-bodied twin who burst from his mother's side and killed her in the birthing, then set himself against his brother's work — twisting the rivers to run crooked, raising thorns and mountains and monsters, marring each good making with a hardness of his own.
figures.facet: The adversary who is kin, not Other: Flint is no separate dark god but the co-creator's own brother, and the world's roughness — its rapids, its briars, its frost — is his signature, evil written into creation as a sibling's stubborn handiwork rather than an invading enemy.
figures.provenance: Sapling/Flint (Tijuskeha/Tawiskaron in Huron-Wyandot; Teharonhiawako/Sawiskera in Mohawk) is widely attested; the degree of the twins' opposition was sharpened in some later tellings by Christian dualist framing — read the 'good vs. evil' polarity with that contact-layer in mind.

figures.gloss: The indwelling spiritual force — the potency that everything possesses in its measure: the hunter and the hunted, the shaman, the storm, the very stones; not a god and not a soul but a power that contends, the orenda of one thing pitted against the orenda of another in every striving of the world.
figures.facet: Holy power as a verb of contention: where 'mana' or 'wakȟáŋ' name a sacred fullness, Hewitt's Iroquois orenda is specifically the power one exerts and bends against another's — magic as a wrestling of potencies, the sacred as the very medium of effort and outcome.

figures.gloss: The constitution given by the Peacemaker (Deganawida) and Hiawatha to bind the Five Nations into one Longhouse — the Tree of Peace planted at Onondaga, its white roots reaching to the four directions, the weapons of war buried beneath; an order of councils, clans and consensus that mirrors the cosmos in its making.
figures.facet: Cosmic order codified as a working polity: where many traditions leave right-order an abstraction, the Haudenosaunee forged it into an actual constitution with named offices and procedures — the Tree of Peace a literal axis-mundi around which a federation of nations is governed, sacred order as functioning statecraft.
figures.provenance: Held in living oral tradition (the recitation can run for days) and recorded in several 19th–20th-c. written codifications; dating of the founding is debated (commonly 12th–15th c.).

figures.gloss: Maize, bean and squash — the three life-supporters grown together as one, the bean climbing the cornstalk and the squash shading their roots; addressed as sisters and spirits, they are thanked in the Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen, the Thanksgiving Address, as the sustainers given for the people's life.
figures.facet: Agriculture itself sacralised as a family of mutual aid: the three crops are not merely useful plants but kindred beings whose interdependence — each helping the others grow — is taught as a model of how the people themselves should live, the cornfield a parable of reciprocity.

edges.note: Both name a pervasive spiritual force/potency present in all things and persons, the animate charge under the visible world; ramat diverges by being the very soul that can leave a body and pass on, where orenda is more the power a being exerts. Parallel animisms, unconnected.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An earthly utopian system defined by the total cessation of cruelty, warfare, and structural hierarchy.
GOLD SEAM: Like the Haudenosaunee Great Peace, a highly structured, legally binding constitutional framework meant to end war forever and unite scattered peoples into one body.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The invisible, potent, and strictly amoral energy of the universe that must be safely managed by specialized individuals.
GOLD SEAM: Both are foundational, non-anthropomorphic conceptions of a pervasive spiritual life-force that animates all phenomena and can be harnessed.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both conceptualize an invisible, animating mental or spiritual force that directly shapes and manipulates physical circumstances.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Direct Iroquoian cultural and linguistic descent linking the foundational maternal figures whose bodies produce the foundational crops of corn and beans.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A shared Iroquoian creation mechanism where an unassuming animal dives into a primordial sea to retrieve the speck of mud that becomes the earth.
GOLD SEAM: An invisible, circulating spiritual currency that empowers beings and objects, expanding through success and dissipating through failure.
GOLD SEAM: An invisible, pervasive supernatural force that can be housed in objects or wielded by the spiritually potent.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent an invisible, transmissible spiritual energy that can be harnessed, gathered, and directed through ritual action.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An invisible, quantifiable spiritual energy that must be carefully accumulated and directed to achieve higher states of being.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The permeating, invisible spiritual energy that concentrates in specific people, objects, and words of power.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both assert an animating spiritual force or sacredness that permeates all things, placing humans within a living web of ecological agency rather than above it.

She falls and the turtle rises to meet her — Sky Woman descending from the broken Sky-World caught and set upon the Great Turtle's back; the falling mother and the bearing beast are the two halves of the single creation-scene.
The dived-up mud is spread upon the shell — the muskrat's pinch of earth grows into the world only because the turtle offers the ground beneath it; diver and platform are one cosmogonic act.

The grandsons of Sky Woman, born to make and to unmake — Sapling shaping straight rivers and ripe fruit, Flint twisting them crooked and raising thorns; the lived world is the standing balance of their contention, divine twins as the engine of creation itself.

The grandmother and her good-minded grandson — Sky Woman's daughter bears the twins, and from her line Teharonhiawako finishes the world she began; the great-mother's earth is completed by the culture-hero of her own blood.

Flint bursts from his mother's side and kills her in the birthing — the adversary's first act is matricide within the founding family; the world's hardness is born, literally, of the great-mother's death.

Two North-American emergence-and-making cosmogonies set side by side — the Iroquois earth dived up from beneath the water against the Navajo First Man and First Woman climbing up through the worlds; both build the lived world from a below, not a sky-command.

The good twin who fits the world for human life beside the Navajo Monster-Slayer who clears it of menace — paired heroic sons in two unrelated Native cosmologies, each making the world habitable, one by shaping and one by slaying.

Both are the second of a creative twin-pair, but the rhyme is loose and even inverted: Tó Bájísh Chíní is Born-for-Water, the water-twin who aids his brother, where Tawiskaron is Flint, the brother's opponent — paired-twin structure shared, moral valence reversed; flagged.

American divine twins who bring order against death and disorder — Sapling and Flint of the Northeast beside Hunahpu and Xbalanque of the Popol Vuh; the contending or cooperating brother-pair as a New-World cosmogonic engine, no shared line between them.

The marrer-from-within the family of the makers — Flint the brother who roughens every good making, Loki the blood-brother of the gods whose mischief frays their order; the adversary as kin rather than external enemy, reached in two unconnected worlds.

Both the obsidian-or-flint-bodied disruptive power set against a brighter creator-brother — Tawiskaron against Sapling, dark Tezcatlipoca against Quetzalcoatl; a loose stone-mirror/flint adversary rhyme across the Americas, flagged as a reach.

Two Native names for a sacred power diffused through all things — Iroquois orenda and Lakota wakȟáŋ both a holy potency in stone, storm and person alike; the one-through-many intuition reached independently across North America.
Orenda and teōtl both name a pervasive sacred energy rather than a personal high god — the impersonal holy power-stuff of the cosmos, arrived at on opposite ends of the continent without contact.

Both point past the gods to a single underlying power, but loosely — Brahman is the absolute ground of being, orenda the contending potency one thing wields against another; the one-through-many family, but orenda is plural and agonistic where Brahman is one and still.

Both make a world-body of the cosmos itself — the land borne on the Turtle's living back, the sky a parent-being overhead in Polynesia; a faint world-as-creature echo only, the figures otherwise unalike, flagged.

The land conceived as a sacred ground reaching to the four directions — Turtle Island bearing the world against the Lakota hoop crossed by the four winds; two Native cosmograms of the earth as an ordered, quartered whole.

The Tree of Peace is planted upon Turtle Island and its white roots run to the four directions of that same land — the political axis-mundi rooted in the cosmological one; the constitution grows from the creation.

Both name a right way the world is meant to follow, but loosely — the Dao a flowing impersonal order, the Great Law a deliberate human covenant modelled on the cosmos; cosmic-order shared, character opposite, flagged.

A holy tree at the world's centre sending its roots or rivers to the four quarters — the Tree of Peace at Onondaga against Eden's tree and four rivers; the axis-tree reaching to the four directions, imagined twice.

The staple harvest sacralised and given a feminine face of plenty — the Three Sisters thanked as living kindred against Demeter the grain-mother of the Mysteries; the cultivated field made holy and motherly in unrelated worlds.

In many tellings the maize, bean and squash spring from the body of Sky Woman's buried daughter — the Three Sisters are the great-mother's own gift, the crops growing from the founding ancestress's grave.

Both answer the question of where solid earth first came from, but by opposite routes — Gaia the primordial Earth simply arising, the muskrat's mud dived up from beneath a prior sea; the earth-diver's contingent, animal-won land set against the self-born Earth, flagged.

Two Native great-mothers from whose persons the people and their sustenance descend — Sky Woman who danced the island wide and bore the human line, Changing Woman who made the first Navajo clans; the ancestress as the source of the nation in two cosmologies.

Two names for a sacred power diffused in differing measure through persons, places, and things — Hawaiian mana and Iroquois orenda; the holy as an efficacious potency rather than a personal god, intuited independently across the Pacific and North America.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A transmissible, deeply localized spiritual power or essence residing in specific individuals, granting them supernatural efficacy.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are strictly impersonal, transmissible forces of spiritual power and efficacy that exist independent of personified deities.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both function as an animating, pervasive spiritual energy or song that empowers individuals to enact change and commune with nature.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both personify the foundational agricultural crops of the Eastern Woodlands as inherently maternal, sacred entities.
GOLD SEAM: Both conceptualize a manipulable, ubiquitous spiritual power that animates the world and can be directed by human intent.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A shared North American mechanism where an unassuming animal dives into a primordial sea to retrieve the speck of mud that becomes the earth.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An invisible, animating spiritual force that descends upon and drastically empowers the practitioner, operating beyond mere anthropomorphic forms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A pervasive, invisible spiritual energy present in all living things that can be tapped by practitioners to influence reality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The personification of vital staple crops that demand deeply respectful, localized cultivation rituals to sustain human life.
GOLD SEAM: An invisible, circulating spiritual power that animates objects, dictates ritual efficacy, and can be amassed or depleted.
GOLD SEAM: Both conceptualize a raw, invisible spiritual power that can be concentrated to produce tangible, physical effects in the material world.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A pervasive spiritual energy that can be tapped into or channeled, operating as a natural law of the universe.

Both are American beginnings where the first people arrive from elsewhere into this world, but the Taíno climb up from a cave while Sky-Woman falls from above — a loose structural rhyme, opposite in direction, flagged as a reach.

The genuine circum-Arctic earth-diver belt: Siberia and the Haudenosaunee muskrat share one ancient cosmogonic motif. A real distributional link of great antiquity — shared deep inheritance/diffusion, not documented contact, so tiered 3 not 1.

Same earth-from-the-deep cosmogony; the Siberian telling diverges by doubling the diver into rivals so the dive births evil, where the Turtle-Island telling makes it an act of consent and self-sacrifice.
Both a diffuse indwelling sacred potency rather than a personal god; kut diverges by binding political legitimacy and the living soul into one substance the sky lends.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both conceptualize a distinct, personal spiritual energy or magical volition that can be cultivated and exerted upon the world.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both treat spiritual energy as a localized, transmissible force that can enact tangible change on the physical and social environment.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The deification of the staple crop, recognizing maize not as a passive resource but as a sacred, reciprocal being.

dossier.essence: The living religion of the Haudenosaunee — the 'People of the Longhouse,' the Iroquois Confederacy of Mohawk, Oneida, Onondaga, Cayuga, Seneca, and (from c. 1722) Tuscarora — of the northeastern North American woodlands. It centers on a creation begun when Sky Woman fell to a water-world and the earth was raised on the Great Turtle's back, on her twin grandsons who together make and mar the world, on a pervasive spiritual potency (orenda), and on structured gratitude to the beings that sustain life.
dossier.cosmos: The cosmos begins above: Sky Woman falls through the hole left by an uprooted celestial tree, is caught by waterfowl, and set on the Great Turtle's back, where a diving muskrat's pinch of mud — won at the cost of its life — grows into the earth, Turtle Island. Her daughter bears twins: Teharonhiawako (Sapling, the Good Mind) shapes a world fitted for life, and Tawiskaron (Flint) roughens it, the lived world their standing balance. All things carry orenda, spiritual power; from 1799 the prophet Handsome Lake added a moral teaching of heaven, judgment, and renewal.
dossier.practice: Longhouse religion runs a calendar of thanksgiving ceremonies — Midwinter (with the stirring of ashes and dream rites), Maple, Planting, Strawberry, Green Corn, and Harvest — each framed by the Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen, the 'words that come before all else,' which greets and thanks every part of creation. Medicine societies (notably the False Face Society, with its carved masks) heal; tobacco is burned to carry prayer; and communal life is ordered by matrilineal clans and by the Great Law of Peace.
dossier.canon: Oral: the Creation story; the Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen (Thanksgiving Address); the Kaianere'kó:wa (Great Law of Peace, the Confederacy's constitution, recited from wampum); and the Gaiwiio ('Good Message') of Handsome Lake, recited annually. Early external records come through the Jesuit Relations (17th c.) and the ethnographies of Lewis Henry Morgan and the Tuscarora scholar J. N. B. Hewitt.
dossier.reflects: Cosmic order as covenant and gratitude — a religion whose founding act is not a command but a falling caught by helpful animals, and whose central rite is the systematic thanking of every being that keeps the people alive.
sources: Lewis Henry Morgan, 'League of the Ho-dé-no-sau-nee, or Iroquois' (1851)
Anthony F. C. Wallace, 'The Death and Rebirth of the Seneca' (1970)
William N. Fenton, 'The Great Law and the Longhouse: A Political History of the Iroquois Confederacy' (1998)
